title: Dog Walking in Circles and Falling Over: Causes and Emergency Steps
meta_desc: Is your dog circling or losing balance? Learn why this is a high-priority emergency and how to help your pet right now.
content_html:
What is happening to my dog?

When a dog begins walking in repetitive circles or repeatedly falling over, it is usually a sign of a disturbance in their vestibular system, which is responsible for balance and spatial orientation. This condition, often called ataxia, can be caused by various issues ranging from deep ear infections and 'Old Dog Vestibular Disease' to more serious concerns like neurological toxins, strokes, or brain tumors.

Is this a medical emergency?

Urgency Level: High. Yes, you should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately. While some causes are not life-threatening, the symptoms of circling and falling can indicate a severe neurological event or poisoning. If your dog also has flickering eye movements (nystagmus), a persistent head tilt, or is unable to stand at all, they require professional stabilization and diagnosis right away.

How a photo or video helps triage

If it is safe to do so, capturing a 15-to-30-second video of your dog's behavior can be an invaluable tool for the veterinary team. A video allows the doctor to see the exact nature of the circling, the direction of any eye flickering, and the specific way your dog is losing balance. This visual evidence helps the vet differentiate between a peripheral issue (like the inner ear) and a central issue (like the brain) much faster than a verbal description alone.

faqs.question: Could my dog have had a stroke?
faqs.answer: While less common in dogs than in humans, strokes can cause sudden loss of balance and circling. A veterinarian will need to perform a neurological exam to confirm the cause.

faqs.question: What is 'Old Dog Vestibular Disease'?
faqs.answer: This is a common, sudden-onset condition in senior dogs that affects balance. While the symptoms look scary and include falling and circling, many dogs recover with supportive care.

faqs.question: What should I do while driving to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog confined in a crate or safely restrained to prevent them from injuring themselves further. Keep the car quiet and dim to reduce stress and nausea.
